title: US embassy in Saudi Arabia closes as Iran retaliates
author: ABC News
fullText: The war with Iran now expanding all across the Middle East. The US embassy in Saudi Arabia closing today after multiple sources say it was hit by Iranian drones. I'm joined now here in studio by Michael Ratney, the former US ambassador to Saudi Arabia. Good to see you again, Michael. I guess can you confirm? We've got multiple sources telling us um that the embassy was hit by Iranian drones. Is that what >> Yeah, it definitely it definitely was struck by two drones last night. There was fortunately no one was hurt. It was the middle of the night, but there was some property damage, and it's obviously pretty unnerving for the staff there. >> Well, indeed. And and so now there have been reports, we have not been able to confirm this, but I wanted to bring it to you, that Saudi may be getting involved now, um in in this uh war against Iran, possibly even launching war plananes. What are your thoughts? >> So, I've heard some of these reports. I'm still somewhat cautious about them. I know why. It's pretty clear that the Iranians are trying to go the Saudis and the other Gulf governments into the fight. Uh they've launched attacks not just on our embassies, but on facilities, including energy facilities throughout the Gulf, including in Saudi Arabia. I feel like either on purpose or implicitly, they're goating the Saudis into the fray. For the Saudis, that would be a big decision, right? Uh, I know this infuriates them what's going on right now, but for them to get into the fight means drawing a reprisal from the Iranians, a very direct reprisal that becomes an almost uncontrollable escalation. We're already at a point now where this war is calling into question the ability of these countries to attract tourists and visitors. You see all these images from the airports of stranded passengers. For this to escalate even further is a risky proposition. And so I think it would be a big decision for the Saudis to actually get into the fight at this point. >> So the war is clearly expanding. Uh we've got correspondents in all over the Middle East now. Um your thoughts about that? Is it going to keep expanding and and what countries could you see getting involved in in a way that the US and Israel is involved? >> Sure. We saw it yesterday and it continues today. Hezbollah in Lebanon was starting to fire rockets into Israel and Israel launched a pretty uh robust reprisal. Uh there's militia groups inside of Iraq and of course the Houthis in Yemen continue to pose a threat to commercial navigation to US naval ships to Saudi Arabia. All of that could certainly escalate and you could see continued Iran Iranian attacks not just on US bases or embassies but on civilian population centers and airports throughout the region. So the potential for a continued escalation is definitely there. To some degree, it's going to depend on the US what the what President Trump's decision is about how long he wants this to last and whether or not and how he could define success and a and a normal end to this conflict. >> I mean, when you were listening to the president speak today, a couple things caught my attention that it it was worrisome. I I think in particular um when he said, um, I'm not really sure who's going to take over. And he said, "Wouldn't you know," and I'm paraphrasing, but wouldn't it be a shame if we did all this and then somebody just as bad as the Ayatollah were to take over the country? I'm just having visions of Iraq and what a nightmare that was. There was no plan B for a government and a smooth transition and we saw what happened there. Um, you know, what do you make of what the president said and does that cause you concern? >> Yeah, it certainly does. I mean, no doubt about it. We and the Israelis are doing considerable damage to the Iranian leadership, to their military capability, to their ballistic missile and nuclear programs. No doubt about that. But it asked the question, what are our ultimate objectives? Where does this lead to? How do we define success? What comes after this Iranian government? There's even this debate about whether it's actually regime change or something else. And the press conference today reinforced that view that we don't have a clear idea of where this goes or how this ends. Um, some reports suggest that he looked at the situation in Venezuela where we essentially took out the leader and then let the existing government continue more or less as it was. >> But Iran is way more complex than that. You've got the military, you've got the clerics, you have I mean >> far far more complex. And even by Trump's own admission, we've we've killed more than just the top layer of that government there. Apparently, even people that we thought might be potential successors to. So, what it tells me is we really don't know where this is going. We don't know who would run Iran uh once the violence stops. And it makes for a very dangerous situation because assuming we do want a ceasefire or we do want some sort of a future relationship, we don't know with whom that would be. >> Stay in close touch. We appreciate all your uh help as we kind of work our way through all this. Thank you so much, Ambassador. Check here.
